1: "If you can find one person who deals honestly and seeks truth, I will forgive Jerusalem." The decisive Christological verse of the chapter — the search for one righteous person. Abraham bargained for Sodom with ten (Genesis 18:32); here YHWH requires only one. The search fails among all humans (cf. Ecclesiastes 7:28 — "I found one upright man among a thousand... but not one upright woman among all these"). Romans 3:10-12 declares "there is no one righteous, not even one." Christ is the one exception the search finds: the single righteous one whose existence makes forgiveness possible (Romans 5:19 — "through the obedience of the one man the many will be made righteous").
2: "Even when they say 'As the LORD lives,' they are swearing falsely." The covenant oath invoked insincerely. Christ never swore falsely — Matthew 5:37: "let your 'Yes' be 'Yes' and your 'No,' 'No.'" He is described as "the Amen, the faithful and true witness" (Revelation 3:14). The false oath-swearing that condemns Israel in Jeremiah is the exact failure Christ does not share — his perfect truthfulness is the ground on which the forgiveness Jeremiah 5:1 seeks can be given.
3: "You struck them, but they felt nothing; you crushed them, but they refused correction." The ineffectual discipline — blows that harden rather than break. Christ alone truly "felt" the blow in both senses: Isaiah 53:4-5 — "he took up our pain and bore our suffering... he was pierced for our transgressions, he was crushed for our iniquities." Where Israel refused correction by the strikes they received, Christ received strikes that were not his and absorbed them with full effect — his suffering accomplished what Israel's suffering refused to do.
4: "These are only the poor; they are ignorant; they do not know the way of the LORD." The charitable first explanation for covenant failure. Jesus was born among the poor and proclaimed good news to them (Luke 4:18; Matthew 11:5). He taught the uneducated fishermen "the way of the LORD" that even the poor could grasp. The ignorance of the poor that Jeremiah excuses is the ignorance Christ addresses with his open-access teaching: "the poor have the good news proclaimed to them."
5: "So I went to the leaders... but they too had broken the yoke and torn off the bonds." Both poor and great fail — the universal failure that Romans 3:9-12 systematizes ("Jews and Gentiles alike are all under the power of sin... there is no one righteous, not even one"). Christ stands outside this universal: he did not break the yoke (he took on the easy yoke, Matthew 11:30) and he did not tear off the bonds of covenant obligation but fulfilled them entirely (Matthew 5:17 — "I have not come to abolish the Law or the Prophets but to fulfill them").
6: Three attackers: lion, wolf, leopard — the threat intensifying. These images of destructive power become in the NT the adversarial forces Christ has overcome. 1 Peter 5:8 (the devil as prowling lion); John 10:12 (the wolf scattering the flock — the hireling runs but the Good Shepherd stays). Christ does not flee from the lion, wolf, or leopard but faces the spiritual powers they represent and at the cross "disarmed the powers and authorities" (Colossians 2:15).
7: "How can I forgive you for this?" The question is rhetorical in Jeremiah — the violations are so comprehensive that forgiveness seems impossible. Romans 3:25-26 answers the question: God "presented Christ as a sacrifice of atonement... so as to be just and the one who justifies those who have faith in Jesus." The apparent impossibility of forgiveness Jeremiah raises is resolved not by overlooking the sin but by the substitutionary atoning death that makes forgiveness both possible and just.
8: "They are like well-fed, lusty stallions — each neighing after another man's wife." The sexual appetite as metaphor for undiscriminating covenant infidelity. James 4:4 uses the same adultery-metaphor: "you adulterous people, don't you know that friendship with the world means enmity against God?" Christ calls the soul to exclusive covenant loyalty — the husband-wife exclusive love that runs through Jeremiah's imagery finds its fulfillment in Christ as the bridegroom (John 3:29; Ephesians 5:25-27) who makes the church his faithful bride.
9: "Should I not call them to account? Should I not take vengeance?" The divine question of justice. Romans 12:19 — "vengeance is mine, I will repay, says the Lord." Christ bears this judgment on behalf of the sinners who deserve it (1 Peter 2:24 — "he himself bore our sins in his body on the cross"). The divine vengeance demanded in Jeremiah 5:9 was executed — not set aside — but it fell on the Son rather than destroying those in him. Justice is satisfied; mercy is provided.
10: "Advance through her vineyards and ravage them, but do not destroy them completely." The vine-ravaging that does not annihilate. John 15:1-2 — the Father as the gardener who removes unfruitful branches but does not destroy the vine itself. Christ as the true vine survives all pruning and ravaging; those who remain in him bear fruit even when the external evidence looks like catastrophe. The incomplete destruction of Jeremiah 5:10 reflects the remnant-principle that Christ embodies as the indestructible vine.
11: "The house of Israel and the house of Judah have acted completely faithlessly toward me." The comprehensive covenant failure of both kingdoms. Christ is the faithful Israel — the one who did not act faithlessly. Matthew 3:15: "it is proper for us to do this to fulfill all righteousness." Where both houses failed at every point, Christ fulfills every obligation as the faithful covenant partner. His faithfulness covers the unfaithfulness; he is the representative faithful Israelite (Galatians 3:16 — "the promises were spoken to Abraham and to his seed... meaning one person, who is Christ").
12: "He will do nothing; no disaster will come upon us." The denial of divine judgment — the practical atheism that ignores YHWH's announced intentions. 2 Peter 3:3-4 describes the same scoffing in the last days: "Where is this 'coming' he promised?" Christ's return will vindicate all the prophets' warnings that were denied — including Jeremiah's. The denial of Jeremiah 5:12 is the prototype of the denial of Christ's return that Peter warns against.
13: "The prophets are nothing but wind; the word of God is not in them." The accusation against the true prophets by those who prefer the false ones. Jesus warned of the same reversal (Matthew 7:15 — "beware of false prophets"; Matthew 7:22-23 — "Lord, Lord, did we not prophesy in your name?"). Christ is himself the Word of God (John 1:1) — the word that the accusers of Jeremiah 5:13 denied was in the true prophets is the word made flesh in Christ. He vindicates the true word by being the ultimate prophet.
14: "I will make my words in your mouth a fire, and this people the wood, and it will consume them." The divine word as consuming fire. Hebrews 4:12 — "the word of God is alive and active, sharper than any two-edged sword." Christ is identified as the Word (John 1:1) and the one whose eyes are like "blazing fire" (Revelation 1:14; Jeremiah 19:12). The fire-word of Jeremiah 5:14 — the word that judges and consumes — is the same word that, in Christ, also creates and purifies (Luke 12:49 — "I have come to bring fire on the earth").
15: "I am bringing a nation against you from far away... an ancient nation, an enduring nation, whose language you do not know." The incomprehensible foreign aggressor as instrument of judgment. In Acts 2:4-11, the gift of tongues reverses the linguistic barrier — nations that spoke incomprehensible languages to each other now hear the mighty acts of God in their own tongues. What was a judgment-sign (unintelligible language of the invader) becomes a blessing-sign (the Spirit-given languages of Pentecost), both occurring in Jerusalem.
16: "Their quivers are like open graves; they are all mighty men." Paul quotes similar language from the Psalms in Romans 3:13 ("their throats are open graves") as part of the catena proving universal human sinfulness. The arrows that kill from open-grave quivers represent the power of death that Christ has defeated (1 Corinthians 15:55 — "where, O death, is your victory?"; Revelation 6:8 — Death and Hades as powers ridden by the pale horse, but ultimately thrown into the lake of fire, Revelation 20:14).
17: The nation devours everything — harvest, food, sons and daughters, flocks, vines, fig trees, fortified cities. The comprehensive devouring of Israel's blessings anticipates the restoration promise in Joel 2:25 — "I will repay you for the years the locusts have eaten." Christ's ministry of provision (feeding thousands in the wilderness, John 6:35 — "I am the bread of life") is the reversal of this devouring — he who is the true harvest cannot be consumed but gives himself as inexhaustible food.
18: "Yet even in those days I will not make a complete end of you." The remnant-promise repeated (cf. Jeremiah 4:27). Romans 9:27-29 quotes Isaiah's remnant-theology alongside Jeremiah's in arguing that God's word has not failed — a remnant is saved. Christ is the true remnant, the single righteous one (Jeremiah 5:1) in whom the remnant-promise finds its fullest form. Those who are "in Christ" share his remnant-status: they are the complete end that was not made because of him.
19: "As you abandoned me and served foreign gods in your own land, so you will serve foreigners in a land not your own." The covenant curse of service-reversal: from serving YHWH to serving foreign gods, from your land to a foreign land. Christ reverses this: Galatians 4:4-5 — "God sent his Son... to redeem those under the law, that we might receive adoption to sonship." The service-reversal of Jeremiah's curse is answered by Christ's redemption that restores sonship (not servitude) and inheritance (not exile).
20: The announcement to all of Jacob and Judah — the proclamation that encompasses both houses. Christ's gospel is also announced to the whole house: Romans 1:16 — "the power of God... first to the Jew, then to the Gentile." The same word that announced judgment to all Jacob in Jeremiah is the word of the gospel that goes to all Jacob's descendants and beyond — the announcement-structure of Jeremiah 5:20 is the structure of apostolic proclamation.
21: "You foolish and senseless people — you have eyes but do not see; you have ears but do not hear." Jesus quotes Isaiah 6:9-10 (the same tradition as Jeremiah 5:21) in explaining why he speaks in parables (Matthew 13:13-15) — and then he opens the disciples' eyes. His healings of the blind (Mark 8:22-26; John 9) are enacted answers to this verse: where the whole nation had eyes that did not see, Christ restores sight both physically and spiritually. The deaf-ear that cannot hear the word is opened by the one whose own ears were perfectly attuned to the Father (John 5:30).
22: "Should you not fear me? Should you not tremble before me? I am the one who set the sand as the boundary for the sea, as an eternal limit it cannot cross." The Creator who set the sea's limit is the God incarnate who commands the sea directly — Mark 4:39: "he rebuked the wind and said to the waves, 'Quiet! Be still!'" And the disciples asked, "Who is this? Even the wind and the waves obey him!" (Mark 4:41). The one who made the sea's boundary in creation sets it again in a single word. Fear of YHWH (Jeremiah 5:22) and wonder at Christ (Mark 4:41) are the same response.
23: "But this people has a stubborn, rebellious heart." The stubborn heart (sōrēr) is the same diagnosis as Ezekiel 36:26 — "I will remove from you your heart of stone and give you a heart of flesh." Christ performs this surgery through the Spirit: 2 Corinthians 3:3 — "you are a letter... written not with ink but with the Spirit of the living God, not on tablets of stone but on tablets of human hearts." The stubborn, rebellious heart of Jeremiah 5:23 is the heart Christ transforms through his new covenant work.
24: "They do not even say in their hearts, 'Let us fear the LORD our God, who gives the autumn and spring rains." The failure to recognize the Creator in his creation-gifts. James 5:7 uses the same rain-agriculture imagery for patience: "the farmer waits for the land to yield its valuable crop, patiently waiting for the autumn and spring rains." And James 5:8 — "You too, be patient and stand firm, because the Lord's coming is near." Christ is the giver of the rains (as YHWH) and the object of patient waiting (as the returning Lord).
25: "Your iniquities have diverted these good things, and your sins have kept them from you." Sin as a barrier to covenant blessing — what was meant to flow is dammed by iniquity. Christ removes the barrier: Romans 5:1-2 — "since we have been justified through faith, we have peace with God... and we rejoice in the hope of the glory of God." The access (prosagōgē) that sin had blocked is restored in Christ. The rain withheld because of sin flows again when the sin-barrier is removed at the cross.
26: "Among my people there are wicked men — they lurk like hunters who set traps." The predatory exploitation of the vulnerable within the covenant community. Jesus describes the same predation in Matthew 23:14 (those who devour widows' houses) and in Luke 4:18 (his mission is to set the oppressed free). His parable of the unmerciful servant (Matthew 18:23-35) — the one forgiven much who refuses to forgive little — describes the same predatory wickedness within the community of grace that Jeremiah indicts.
27: "As a cage is full of birds, so their houses are full of goods gotten by deceit." The accumulated wealth of exploitation — the cage-full of stolen abundance. Luke 12:16-21 (the parable of the rich fool) and Luke 16:19-31 (the rich man and Lazarus) are Christ's parables of ill-gotten wealth with the same verdict as Jeremiah 5:27-28. The cage-full of fraudulently obtained goods in Jeremiah corresponds to the barns full of grain in the parable — and the same divine verdict applies: "You fool! This very night your life will be demanded from you."
28: "They do not defend the rights of the poor; they do not plead the cause of the needy." The failure of social covenant justice — the very criterion of true religion (Isaiah 1:17; Micah 6:8). James 1:27 — "religion that God our Father accepts as pure and faultless is this: to look after orphans and widows." Christ's announcement in Matthew 25:45 — "whatever you did not do for the least of these, you did not do for me" — is the final judgment on the failure Jeremiah describes. Christ is both the ultimate Judge who asks the question and, in the poor, the one whose cause is not defended.
29: "Should I not take vengeance on a nation such as this?" The divine justice-question, repeated from Jeremiah 5:9. Paul's answer (Romans 3:25-26): God presented Christ as the mercy seat — he demonstrated his justice by not letting sin go unpunished (Christ bore the punishment) and demonstrated his mercy by providing righteousness to those who trust in Jesus. The divine vengeance of Jeremiah 5:29 is satisfied; the divine mercy of the gospel is simultaneously established. Christ resolves the justice-mercy tension the question raises.
30: "A horrible and shocking thing has occurred in the land." The summary introduction before the false-prophets indictment of Jeremiah 5:31. The same shock vocabulary — "horrible thing" — marks the ultimate horror in the crucifixion narrative: the death of the Son of God is the most horrible and shocking thing that has occurred. Yet that horror becomes the source of salvation. The shocking things of covenant history accumulate toward the final shocking event of the cross.
31: "The prophets prophesy what is false, the priests exercise authority on their own terms, and my people love it this way." The collapse of the prophetic and priestly offices into self-serving corruption — and the people's complicity in preferring comfortable lies. Jesus speaks to this directly: Matthew 7:15-23 — false prophets in sheep's clothing; "I never knew you." He is the true Prophet (Deuteronomy 18:15-18; Acts 3:22) who speaks only what the Father tells him (John 12:49) and the true Priest who does not serve on his own terms but was appointed by YHWH (Hebrews 5:4-6). He replaces the corrupt prophetic and priestly offices with his own perfect double-ministry.
